coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with chiral surface selectively inhibit the activity of laccase
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiting
Zhang, Mengling
Ma, Yurong
Wu, Jie
Wang, Yun
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Chiral carbon dots (CDots) have received much attention in biocatalysis due to their biocompatibility and low toxicity. For chiral CDots-enzyme hybrid system, great efforts are needed to understand the interactions between chiral CDots and enzymes. Here, chiral CDots (L-/D-Trp-CDots) from citric acid and L-/D-tryptophan were designed and synthesized. The obtained L-Trp-CDots were used to regulate the activity of laccase, showing an 87.26 % inhibition rate of laccase, which is significantly higher than that of D-Trp-CDots (57.84 %) under the same reaction conditions. It is chiral functional groups rather than the carbon core play the important role in chiral CDots for regulating enzymatic activity in this system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Enzyme
Inhibition rate
Chiral functional groups

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of achiral and chiral CDots
Activity assays of laccase and laccase/CDots
Sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)
The analysis of Michaelis - Menten equation for laccase

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 , 52041202 ‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Agricultural science and technology innovation project of Suzhou Science and Technology Development Plan (SNG2020074), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, Ningxia Autonomous Region flexible introduction of science and technology innovation team (2021RXTDLX08) and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of L-Trp-CDots (Inset: particle size distribution of L-Trp-CDs). (b) FT-IR spectra of L-Trp-CDots (red line) and L-Trp (black line). (c) PL excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of L-Trp-CDots. (d) UV–Vis spectra of L-Trp-CDots (red line) and L-Trp (black line). (e) CD spectra of L-Trp (black line) and D-Trp (red line). (f) CD spectra of L-Trp-CDots (black line) and D-Trp-CDots (red line).

The chiral stability of L- and D-Trp-CDots with (a) various pH values (4–10); (b) various temperatures (25–65 °C); (c) various ionic strengths (0.4–2.0 mol L-1 NaCl); and (d) HUVEC viability after 24 h of treatment with various concentrations of L- and D-Trp-CDots.
(a) The full-scan XPS spectra of L-/D-Trp-CDots. High-resolution XPS spectra of L/D -Trp-CDots: (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and (d) O 1 s.

(a) The schematic diagram of the structures of CA-CDots and L-/D-Trp-CDots as changed by chiral functional groups, the number of chiral functional groups of L-/D-Trp-CDots is 26–37 on a carbon core; Substrate degradation of UV–Vis absorbance in a water bath at 30 °C for laccase, LLTCDs (b), LCACDs (c), and LDTCDs (d). (The concentrations of Laccase and L- or D- Trp-CDs are 40 and 0.5 mg mL−1, respectively.)

(a) The inhibition ratio of L-Trp-CDots (red trace) and D-Trp-CDots (blue trace) with various concentrations (0.2–3 mg mL−1). (b) SDS-PAGE analysis of free laccase, LLTCDs, and LDTCDs hybrids; (c) Lineweaver-Burk plots of free laccase, LLTCDs, and LDTCDs (The concentrations of laccase and CDots are 40 mg/mL and 3 mg/mL); (d) CD spectra of free laccase, LLTCDs and LDTCDs (the chiral CDots are used as background liquid to eliminate the influence of their chirality on the CD signals of laccase). (CDots: Laccase = 1:13, Mean ± SD, *P < 0.05 and ***P < 0.001).
The scheme for the synthesis of chiral CDots.
Schematic diagram of enzyme structure changed by chiral CDots.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with chiral surface selectively inhibit the activity of laccase

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chiral CDots were designed by introducing the chiral functional groups on the surface of the carbon core, while the major role for regulating enzymatic activity in chiral CDots is chiral functional groups rather than the carbon core.

Chiral carbon dots (CDots) have received much attention in biocatalysis due to their biocompatibility and low toxicity. For chiral CDots-enzyme hybrid system, great efforts are needed to understand the interactions between chiral CDots and enzymes. Here, chiral CDots (L-/D-Trp-CDots) from citric acid and L-/D-tryptophan were designed and synthesized. The obtained L-Trp-CDots were used to regulate the activity of laccase, showing an 87.26 % inhibition rate of laccase, which is significantly higher than that of D-Trp-CDots (57.84 %) under the same reaction conditions. It is chiral functional groups rather than the carbon core play the important role in chiral CDots for regulating enzymatic activity in this system.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral structures have played a crucial role in biochemistry, medicine and pharmacy fields [1–3]. Chiral carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon fibers have been widely studied in catalysis field [4,5]. Notably, chiral carbon dots (CDots) have received much attention in enzyme catalysis due to their good biocompatibility and low toxicity [6,7].

The nano-hybrid-enzyme system is an ideal catalytic system for biocatalysis, which integrates various advantages from nanocatalysis to heterogeneous catalysis [8,9]. For chiral CDots-enzyme hybrid system, great efforts are needed to understand the interaction between chiral CDots and enzymes. It still needs to study the role of carbon core and chiral functional groups on the enzyme activity regulation [10].

In terms of the formation of chiral CDots, there are two major synthesis strategies for chiral carbon nanostructures: one-step synthesis method and two-step synthesis method. One-step method includes one-step hydrothermal method, microwave method, electrolytic method and so on. For example, Hu et al. fabricated L-/D-Cys-CDots by one-step method from L-/D-cysteine, for which, the L-Cys-CDots could increase laccase activity and the D-Cys-CDots could inhibit laccase activity [11]. Zhang et al. synthesized the chiral CDots from glutamic acid by electrolytic method to inhibit the activity of maltase, and D-Glu-CDots have higher suppressive efficiency than that of L-Glu-CDots [12]. The two-step synthesis method is mainly based on chiral modification on achiral carbon core. The chirality comes from the modification by chiral ligands. For example, Li et al. [13] prepared chiral carbon nanostructures by pyrolyzing citric acid to form carbon core and then modifying them with L-/D-cysteine via covalent coupling of amide bond. Yuan et al. [14] also pyrolyzed citric acid to form carbon core while used L-/D-penicillamine as a chiral source. However, two-step method can control CDots’ chirality by regulating the species and quantity of chiral ligands. So, for clarifying the chiral source and controlling the chirality of CDots, the chiral functional groups modified on achiral CDots by two-step method is better than that by one-step method.

Herein, the citric acid and L-/D-tryptophan (L-/D-Trp) were used as raw materials to prepare chiral CDots (L-/D-Trp-CDots) by a two-step thermal polymerization method. The chirality of these CDots is derived from their surface functional groups. With laccase or laccase/CDots hybrids as catalyst and 2, 2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) as substrate, the inhibitory efficiency on laccase activity was investigated in terms of inhibition ratio and Michaelis constant (Km). Notably, L-Trp-CDots show an 87.26 % inhibition rate of laccase, while that of D-Trp-CDots is only about 57.84 %. L-Trp-CDots possess a worse binding ability to ABTS, which makes the L-Trp-CDots exhibit a stronger inhibition effect than that of D-Trp-CDots. This work indicates, in the present system, it is the chiral functional groups in chiral CDots rather than carbon core that regulate the enzyme activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of achiral and chiral CDots
ce:section.ce:para: The CDots were obtained by the thermal polymerization method [15]. First, 2.0 g citric acid powder was added into the round-bottom flask, and the carbon core (CA-CDots, which is achiral core) was obtained in an oil bath at 180 °C for 50 min. Then 0.5 g L-/D-Trp was added into the round-bottom flask at 180 °C, which was heated and stirred for 30 min. After reaction, 20 mL Milli-Q water was added and the solution was cool to room temperature. To eliminate impurities, the solution was dialyzed for three days using a 500–1000 Da cellulose dialysis membrane. Finally, chiral CDots (L-/D-Trp-CDots) were obtained.

Laccase powder (0.4 g) was agitated for 24 h at 4 °C in 10 mL phosphate buffer solution (PB, 0.01 mol L-1, pH = 6.0). In order to remove the insoluble part, the solution was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 20 min. Then, 0.1 mL of the above laccase solution was combined with 1 mL various doses of CA-CDots (0–1.5 mg mL−1) and L- or D-Trp-CDots (0–3 mg mL−1) by stirring at 4 °C for 24 h, and the laccase/CDots hybrids were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity assays of laccase and laccase/CDots
ce:section.ce:para:
As an oxidation substrate, ABTS was employed to evaluate the enzyme's catalytic capacity [16]. In the assays for evaluating the laccase activity, 10 μL laccase (40 mg mL−1), laccase/CA-CDots (LCACDs), and laccase/L-Trp-CDots (LLTCDs) or laccase/D-Trp-CDots (LDTCDs) solution were immediately combined with a 200 μL substrate solution (0.5 mM) respectively. The combination was incubated at 25 °C for 30 min, and then the OD (optical density) under 417 nm ultraviolet light (OD417) of the combination was evaluated at 30 °C. The following equation was used to determine the inhibition rate:
I n h i b i t i o n r a t i o =
OD
control
-
OD
sample
/
OD
control
× 100 %

Herein,
OD
control
and
OD
sample
represent the OD417 of the analysis system with the laccase and laccase/CDots hybrids as the catalyst, respectively.

Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) were chosen for the cytotoxicity tests of the chiral CDots [17]. The HUVEC (1*104 cells/well) were incubated in the 96 well plate for 24 h and treated with chiral CDots with a series of concentrations (0–3 mg mL−1) for 12 h. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) was used to measure the relative viability of cell samples after 12 h of incubation by examining the metabolic activity of cells treated as indicated above. The cell viability was estimated using the formula below:
V =
VO
D
'
450
VOD
450
× 100 %

Where V denotes cell viability and VOD’450 denotes the OD450 of culture media containing varying doses of CDots and VOD450 is the OD450 value of culture media without CDots. All experiments about cells were performed in compliance with the Soochow University (China) on animal use and ethics (SYXK (SU) 2017–0043).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)
ce:section.ce:para: SDS-PAGE (w/w: 8%), including 1% β-mercapto ethano and 1% lithium dodecyl sulfate (LDS) in the sample loading buffer, was used to separate all the samples at a constant voltage of 80 V for 2 h [18]. Then the gels were dyed with Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250 at 0.25 % concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of Michaelis - Menten equation for laccase
ce:section.ce:para: The Michaelis–Menten equation reflects one of the simplest and best-known model of enzyme kinetics. Substrates with series of concentrations were reacted with laccase or laccase/CDots hybrids, and the initial reaction velocity of varied laccase concentrations was determined. A Lineweaver–Burk plot by graphing 1/V (the reciprocal of starting response velocity) with 1/[S] (the reciprocal of substrate concentration) was made, where the ordinate intercept is 1/Vmax and the abscissa intercept was the value of 1/Km. Finally, the values of Vmax and Km were calculated.

To guarantee the correctness of the results, all measurements were repeated at least three times. In the activity assay of laccase and laccase/CDots, the test was independently performed three times, and the data were evaluated by the Statistical Analysis technique with an analysis of variance and the significant difference t-test. To assess significant differences, a probability threshold of P < 0.05 was employed.

Typically, as shown in Scheme 1
, the synthetic route of the chiral CDots was divided into two steps. In the first step, the citric acid was carbonized at 180 °C for 50 min, and the carbon core was produced. For the second step, the L-/D- tryptophan was introduced into the solution by heating and stirring for 30 min continually under the same temperature. Carbonized citric acid and aromatic tryptophan were bonded through amide bond, and the achiral carbon core was modified by chiral functional groups [19].

Fig. 1
a is the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of L-Trp-CDots. The diameter of L-Trp-CDots ranges from 3 to 7 nm, most of which are 5 nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum is commonly used to examine the sample's surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. S2 , for the FT-IR spectrum of CA-CDots, the stretching vibration signals of O-H at 3434 cm−1, C = O at 1633 cm−1, and C-O at 1390 cm−1 are shown, respectively [12]. Then, the L/D-tryptophan was added to synthesize the chiral CDots. The FTIR spectra of L-Trp-CDots and the raw material L-Trp are shown in Fig. 1 b. The vibration signal of L-Trp-CDots is weaker than that of L-Trp. The stretching vibration of O-H or N-H is assigned to the peak at 3427 cm−1 for L-Trp-CDots, whereas the stretching vibration of the C = O is assigned to the peak at 1710 cm−1 [20,21]. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of the C-N is represented by the peak at 1398 cm−1 [22]. The difference between the two kinds of CDots is that the chiral CDots have new N-H and C-N bonds, which demonstrates that the chiral functional groups are modified on the carbon core and the surface of the chiral CDots is primarily composed of hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amide groups.

Photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were used to investigate the optical characteristics of L-Trp-CDots. L-Trp-CDots exhibit an emission peak at 456 nm (Fig. 1 c, red line) with excitation light of 373 nm (Fig. 1 c, black line) [23,24]. Besides, Fig. 1 d exhibits the UV–Vis absorption spectra of L-Trp-CDots and L-Trp. L-Trp-CDots show absorption in the UV–Vis range (Fig. 1 d, red line). Compared with L-Trp (Fig. 1 d, black line), the absorption peak at 216 nm and 279 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively, are inherited from the L-Trp [25]. In addition, the D-Trp-CDots ( Fig. S1
1c-d) have similar properties of UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence. The circular dichroism (CD) spectrum is used to study the formation of CD handedness [26]. CA-CDots were obtained from citric acid, which are rich in carboxyl groups. According to Fig. S7 , the CA-CDots as carbon core are achiral. And then, the L/D-tryptophan as chiral ligand was added to modify the carbon core. For free Trp, as shown in Fig. 1 e, the CD signals are located at 205 nm and 225 nm [27]. As shown in Fig. 1 f, notably, the mirror image for the CD signals of the synthesized L-Trp-CDots and D-Trp-CDots can be observed. The CD spectra of L- or D-Trp-CDots are noticeably different from those of Trp, especially the CD signals of L- or D-Trp-CDots at 225 nm, which exhibit the opposite optical rotation to the Trp. It is worth noting that, compared with the chiral source, the opposite optical rotation of CD signals is due to the formation of the rigid structure generated by the π conjugation between the aromatic nucleus of the chiral source and the carbon core of CDots. L-Trp may be connected to the carbonated citric acid via amido bonds [19]. The other new symmetrical broad CD signals emerge at the range from 275 to 310 nm, suggesting that a new chiral center is generated.

The stability of the synthesized L-Trp-CDots and D-Trp-CDots at various pH, temperatures, and ionic strength was evaluated to validate their applicability in biological systems. As demonstrated in Fig. 2
a-c, there is no significant variation in the intensities of the chiral signal at 218 nm under different pH, temperature, and ion concentration, suggesting chiral CDots possess the high stability [28]. Fig. S4 shows the chiral signals of L- or D-Trp-CDots at different pH from 4 to 10. When the pH rises from 4 to 10, the positions and intensities of peaks remain unchanged, indicating that the L-Trp-CDots and D-Trp-CDots have good resistance of acid and alkali properties. As shown in Fig. S5 , when the chiral CDots were treated with different temperatures and different ionic strength, the positions and intensities of the chiral signals change weakly. With the increase of temperature and the ion concentration , the signal intensities increase and the peak positions show red-shift about 3 nm. The high temperature and high ion concentration do not affect on the chiral signal, to some extent, indicating that the chiral CDots have good stability. Biocompatibility is also critical in terms of good biocatalysts [29]. Fig. 2 d depicts the metabolic activity of HUVEC treated with various chiral CDots concentrations. Even at the ultra-high concentration of 3 mg mL−1, the cell development and proliferation are unaffected, showing the synthesized chiral CDots are biocompatible enough and could be utilized in biological applications.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) verified the elemental composition and specific chemical bonding of chiral CDots. The full-scan spectra of the L-/D-Trp-CDots are shown in Fig. 3
a, which reveals that CDots are composed of C, N, and O elements with contents of 51.54%, 11.96%, and 36.59%, respectively (Table S1). The C 1 s spectrum of L-Trp-CDots can be deconvoluted into three peaks, as shown in Fig. 3 b. The first peak located at 284.80 eV is attributed to C–C sp3, and the two peaks located at 286.87 and 288.97 eV indicate the presence of C–O/C–N and C = O, respectively. What’s more, the peaks corresponding to pyrrolic (400.15 eV) N and graphitic N (401.50 eV) can be observed in the deconvoluted spectra of N 1 s (Fig. 3 c) [30,31]. Furthermore, for O 1 s (Fig. 3 d), two peaks with binding energies of 532.10 eV and 533.54 eV, correspond to C = O and C-O-C, respectively [32]. From the comparison of the top and bottom graphs in Fig. 3, it can be seen that except for the different chirality, the element composition and chemical bonds of the two kinds of chiral CDots are the same. Based on the above characterization results, it can be shown that the surface of the carbon core is modified by chiral functional groups.

According to the element content of XPS, it is calculated that the structural formula of L-/D-Trp-CDots should be that there are 26–37 chiral functional groups on a carbon core (Fig. 4
a). To regulate the activity of laccase, chiral CDots with opposite optical activity were combined with laccase. In the following experiments, ABTS was employed as an oxidation substrate to assess the catalytic activity of laccase, with all reaction and test conditions remaining constant [33]. To confirm whether the chiral CDots have effects on the oxidation of ABTS, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of ABTS (0.5 mM) and ABTS supplemented with chiral CDots were measured. Fig. S6
a shows that the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of ABTS treated with and without chiral CDs at 25 °C for 30 min. The above absorbance spectra are the same, indicating that chiral CDots do not influence on the oxidation process of ABTS. Therefore, the catalytic activity of ABTS-added laccase, LLTCDs, LDTCDs, and LCACDs may be calculated by graphing the UV–Vis absorbance peak at 417 nm with varying reaction durations (Fig. 4 b-d). In Fig. 4 c, achiral CDots do not affect on the oxidation process of laccase-catalysed ABTS. However, the red and blue shadow areas in Fig. 4 b and Fig. 4 d represent the inhibitory effect of chiral CDots on the enzyme and the red shadow area is larger than the blue shaded area. The addition of chiral CDots reduces the oxidation products produced by laccase-catalyzed ABTS, and the L-Trp-CDots have a better inhibitory effect on laccase than that of the D-Trp-CDots. It can be seen that the activity of laccase is regulated by chiral functional groups.

To properly investigate the effects of chiral CDots with various concentrations on laccase activity, the inhibition ratio of chiral CDots with various concentrations (0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3 mg mL−1) was measured when the reaction time reached 30 min (Fig. 5
a). The inhibition ratio of LLTCDs (red trace) and LDTCDs (blue trace) steadily increase with increasing concentration. When the concentration of L-Trp-CDots increase to 3 mg mL−1, the inhibitory efficiency to laccase reaches 87.26%, which is greater than that of D-Trp-CDots (57.84%). L-Trp-CDots are more effective in inhibiting laccase activity, indicating that the chirality of the CDots affects laccase activity.

Besides, in terms of inhibition of laccase or other enzymes by chiral molecules or particles, Hu et al. [11] proved the L-Cys-CDots could improve the activity of the enzyme up to 20.2 %, whereas the D-Cys-CDots decreased the activity to 10.4%. And Zhang et al. [12] found the inhibition ratio of maltase/LCDots reached 80 % approximately. Here, the inhibitory efficiency of L-Trp-CDots to laccase reached 87.26 %, which is at a high level in recent reports [11,12].

To further confirm the binding of L-Trp-CDots or D-Trp-CDots with laccase, SDS-PAGE test, dynamic light scattering (DLS) detection, and CD spectrum for laccase and laccase L-/D-Trp-CDots hybrids were analysed [34,35]. The results of the SDS-PAGE test can be seen in Fig. 5 b, where the hybrids are slower than that of the free laccase, suggesting that the combination of laccase and L-/D-Trp-CDots was achieved and the D-Trp-CDots interact more effectively with laccase than the L-Trp-CDots. DLS detection was utilized to measure the hydrodynamic diameter of laccase (black line) and laccase/Trp-CDots (LLTCDs: red line; LDTCDs: blue line) to further elucidate the relevant combination. As demonstrated in Fig. S6
b, the size of pure laccase is small than laccase/CDots hybrids. Between the hydrodynamic diameter of LLTCDs and LDTCDs, the size of LDTCDs changes more than that of LLTCDs. These results show that chiral CDots are coupled with laccase.

The Michaelis–Menten kinetics of laccase, LLTCDs, and LDTCDs were then examined and studied to explore how the chiral CDots impact laccase activity. The concentrations of laccase and L-Trp-CDots or D-Trp-CDots are 40 mg mL−1 and 3 mg mL−1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 c, Michaelis constant (Km) reflects the enzyme's binding effectiveness with the substrate [36]. The Michaelis constant (Km) means the concentration of the substrate (S) when the enzymatic reaction reaches half the maximum speed (Vmax). In other words, the Km of laccase hybrids is higher, while the binding ability of the enzyme and the substrate is weaker. Lineweaver–Burk plot analysis was used to calculate the Km and Vmax constant for each kind of enzyme [37]. The slope and intercept data were calculated to obtain the values of Km and Vmax. The Km of Laccase, LLTCDs, and LDTCDs are 0.1403, 0.4220, and 0.2124 mmol L-1, respectively, and Vmax values of them are 0.0339, 0.0019, and 0.0152 mmol L-1 min−1, respectively. The Km of LLTCDs and LDTCDs increase whereas the Vmax of LLTCDs and LDTCDs decrease. According to Table S3, the value of Km for LLTCDs is highest among others laccase hybrids. As a result, L-Trp-CDots possess a worse binding ability to ABTS, which makes the L-Trp-CDots exhibit a stronger inhibition.

At the same time, the line of laccase and the line of LLTCDs or LDTCDs are intersected with the second quadrant, which indicates the L-Trp-CDots or D-Trp-CDots are the mixed inhibitor (noncompetitive-competitive inhibition) for laccase. Besides, compared LLTCDs or LDTCDs with laccase, Vmax of LLTCDs decreases more than that of LDTCDs, suggesting LLTCDs have lower catalytic efficiency than LDTCDs. For Km, the value of LLTCDs is larger than that of LDTCDs, meaning that LLTCDs own the weaker affinity towards the ABTS, resulting in an adverse impact for the catalytic reaction. Overall, LLTCDs have a greater inhibitory effect than LDTCDs.

The secondary structure of laccase and laccase/CDots hybrids was next assessed by CD spectrum (190–250 nm) (Fig. 5 d). Compared with the CD signals of free laccase (black line), the intensity of the positive peak at 197 nm (α-helix) for LLTCDs decreases, and the intensity of the negative peaks at 216 (β-sheet) and 230 nm (random coil) for LDTCDs decreases [38], indicating that the chiral CDots change the structure of laccase and the structure of laccase treated with D-Trp-CDots changes more [39].

Based on all the above results, we further show the proposed mechanism (shown in scheme 2 ) for chiral CDots to inhibit selectively the activity of laccase. Briefly, the L-/D-Trp-CDots with chirality functional groups cause changes in the spatial configuration of laccase, resulting in selective control of laccase activity. Specifically, the inhibition efficiency of L-Trp-CDots is higher than that of D-Trp-CDots, which is due to the non-covalent bond between D-Trp-CDots and laccase being weaker than that of L-Trp-CDots with laccase. And then the LLTCDs hybrids show a higher Michaelis constant Km value and a smaller Vmax value than that of LDTCDs hybrids.

In conclusion, we have acquired a pair of chiral CDots by thermal polymerization from citric acid (carbon source) and L-/D-Trp (chiral source). The structural analysis of the CDots reveals that the chirality was introduced by chiral functional groups on the surface of the carbon core. Furthermore, the inhibition efficiency of L-Trp-CDots is 87.26%, higher than that of D-Trp-CDots (57.84%), which is because that the L-/D-Trp-CDots change the secondary structure of laccase with different degrees. Besides, compared with the LDTCDs, the affinity of LLTCDs to the substrate is weaker, which can be proved by the larger value of Michaelis constant Km and smaller Vmax of LLTCDs. This work provides a basis for understanding the chirality of chiral CDots synthesized by two-step thermal polymerization, whose chirality comes from functional groups rather than carbon core. In addition, this work also contributes to understand the interaction between chiral structure and enzymes.

Xiting Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Mengling Zhang: Formal analysis. Yurong Ma: Investigation, Software. Jie Wu: Software. Yun Wang: Software. Hui Huang: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Agricultural science and technology innovation project of Suzhou Science and Technology Development Plan (SNG2020074), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, Ningxia Autonomous Region flexible introduction of science and technology innovation team (2021RXTDLX08) and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.152540.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
